documents:
Variables in C#

A variable is a named storage location that holds data in memory. Every variable has a data type, a name, and a value.

Before a variable can be used, it must be declared.

Examples:

int age = 20;
double price = 15.99;
string name = "Alice";
bool isStudent = true;
char grade = 'A';

Common data types:

int - whole numbers
double - decimal numbers
string - text
char - single character
bool - true or false

Variables can change their value during program execution.

Example:

int score = 10;
score = 25;

Using meaningful variable names makes programs easier to understand.

Good examples:

studentName
totalPrice
gameScore

Poor examples:

x
a
temp

Constants store values that should never change.

Example:

const double PI = 3.14159;

Object-Oriented Programming

Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) organizes software using objects.

The four main principles are:

Encapsulation
Inheritance
Polymorphism
Abstraction

A class defines the structure of an object.

An object is an instance of a class.

Example:

class Student
{
    public string Name;
    public int Age;
}

Student s = new Student();

Objects contain fields, properties, and methods.

OOP makes large programs easier to maintain and extend.

Collections

Collections store groups of objects.

The most common collection is List<T>.

Example:

List<string> names = new List<string>();

Adding items:

names.Add("Alice");

Removing items:

names.Remove("Alice");

Collections can be searched, sorted, and iterated.

Useful collections include:

List<T>
Dictionary<TKey, TValue>
HashSet<T>
Queue<T>
Stack<T>

Choose the correct collection depending on the problem.

LINQ

LINQ stands for Language Integrated Query.

It allows querying collections using readable syntax.

Example:

var adults = people.Where(p => p.Age >= 18);

Common LINQ methods:

Where()
Select()
OrderBy()
First()
FirstOrDefault()
Any()
Count()

LINQ improves readability and reduces loops.

Example:

var names = students
    .Where(s => s.Age >= 18)
    .Select(s => s.Name);

LINQ works with arrays, lists, and many other collections.

JSON in C#

JSON is a text format for storing and exchanging data.

C# provides System.Text.Json.

Serialization converts an object into JSON.

Example:

string json = JsonSerializer.Serialize(student);

Deserialization converts JSON back into an object.

Example:

Student student =
    JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Student>(json);

JSON is commonly used in:

Web APIs

Configuration files

Data storage

Communication between applications

JSON is lightweight, readable, and widely supported.
